summary: Filled all planning artifacts with concrete V1 scope: CLI tool scanning Express/Fastify/NestJS codebases, producing capability manifests, compiling tools, and serving via local MCP. Awaiting human approval to open planning gate.

decisions.statement: V1 is scoped to the discovery-manifest-compile-serve pipeline for 3 Node.js frameworks only. All 12 vision domains are deferred to V2+.
decisions.rationale: The vision is deliberately broad but V1 must ship a complete, testable vertical slice. Express, Fastify, and NestJS cover the majority of production Node.js API frameworks. Going deep on one ecosystem beats going shallow on many.

decisions.statement: MCP serve is describe-only in V1 — no live API proxying.
decisions.rationale: Live proxy requires auth forwarding, error normalization, and reliability handling. These are significant concerns that would delay V1. Describe-only MCP still proves the pipeline end-to-end and is useful for agent discovery.

decisions.statement: Capability grouping starts as 1:1 route-to-capability mapping. Intelligent grouping deferred.
decisions.rationale: Smart capability grouping (merging related endpoints into higher-level capabilities) is the core vision differentiator but requires heuristic tuning against real codebases. Starting 1:1 lets us ship and iterate based on real feedback.

decisions.statement: No plugin API in V1. Framework support is hardcoded for Express, Fastify, NestJS.
decisions.rationale: Premature abstraction of plugin interfaces risks building the wrong API. V1 hardcodes 3 frameworks; the plugin boundary emerges from the patterns we discover during implementation.

decisions.statement: TypeScript/JavaScript ecosystem only in V1. Python, Go, Java are V2+.
decisions.rationale: Single AST parser ecosystem (ts-morph or equivalent) lets us go deep on inference quality. Multi-language support multiplies the scanning surface without adding core product value.

summary: Tightened the v0.1.0 launch narrative around verified proof points and removed unverified public-install positioning from launch-facing assets.

decisions.statement: I did not accept the prior QA turn's broader launch-consistency conclusion because launch-facing assets still implied a public global install path that had not been validated in the shipped surface.
decisions.rationale: QA re-proved implementation health, but launch truth also depends on CTA accuracy. The in-repo package metadata shows the package is still marked private, so marketing copy needed to stop implying a confirmed public install channel.

decisions.statement: The launch narrative should lead with reviewed manifest output, approved tool JSON, provenance, and an inspectable describe-only MCP surface as the concrete v0.1.0 proof stack.
decisions.rationale: Those claims map directly to the verified implementation and reduce category drift toward execution-engine or hosted-platform promises that are not shipped in v0.1.0.

decisions.statement: Until distribution is explicitly confirmed, early-adopter CTA should be repo-local evaluation on supported Express, Fastify, or NestJS codebases rather than a public package-manager install promise.
decisions.rationale: This keeps launch messaging truthful while still giving qualified users a concrete next step to try the product and generate useful feedback.

summary: Formally specified side_effect_class rules and introduced sensitivity_class as a new canonical artifact field with 5 classification levels (unknown/public/internal/confidential/restricted), V1 defaults to unknown, V2 inference planned.

decisions.statement: sensitivity_class field added to the canonical artifact (tusq.manifest.json and tusq-tools/*.json) with 5 levels: unknown, public, internal, confidential, restricted. V1 defaults to 'unknown' for all capabilities, matching the conservative-default pattern used for input/output schemas.
decisions.rationale: The vision (VISION.md line 57) explicitly requires 'side effects and sensitivity class' in the canonical artifact. Adding the field now with a safe default establishes the contract for downstream consumers without overclaiming inference accuracy. Humans can manually set sensitivity during manifest review.

decisions.statement: Side effects and sensitivity are modeled as two separate fields (side_effect_class + sensitivity_class) rather than a single combined classification. Side effect answers 'does this mutate state?' while sensitivity answers 'what kind of data does this touch?'
decisions.rationale: The vision mentions 'financial' and 'security-sensitive' alongside read/write/destructive. Conflating mutation type with data sensitivity in one field creates ambiguity (e.g., a POST /payments is both a 'write' and 'confidential'). Two fields let agent runtimes make orthogonal decisions: invocation gates (side effect) vs. audit/redaction rules (sensitivity).

decisions.statement: Challenged the previous turns' lack of formal side_effect_class documentation. While the field was implemented in V1 code (classifySideEffect in src/cli.js), the classification rules, agent implications, and design rationale were not formally specified in the planning artifacts. Now documented.
decisions.rationale: The SYSTEM_SPEC listed side_effect_class values but did not explain the classification logic, the handler/path name matching for destructive detection, or why the system is intentionally conservative (preferring false positives over false negatives).

decisions.statement: Planning signoff maintained as Approved: YES. All 4 required artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Status set to needs_human because the planning_signoff gate requires human approval.
decisions.rationale: Gate requirement: planning_signoff requires human approval. All artifacts updated and consistent.

verification.evidence_summary: Verified sensitivity_class field appears in capability object shape, field table, compiled tool shape, and classification rules section. Verified side_effect_class classification rules are formally documented with agent implications. All 4 planning artifacts exist and are internally consistent.

decisions.rationale: That ordering keeps the launch legible on a skim and preserves the narrow, defensible v0.1.0 promise.

summary: Formally specified auth and permission expectations in the canonical artifact: detection rules, agent implications, V1 limitations, and V2 structured auth_requirements shape. Completes the three-field governance model (side_effect_class + sensitivity_class + auth_hints).

decisions.statement: Challenged prior turns for leaving auth_hints under-specified. The field shipped in V1 code (inferAuthHints in src/cli.js) but was never formally documented with detection rules, agent implications, or V1 limitations in the planning artifacts. VISION.md line 58 explicitly requires 'auth and permission expectations' as a core canonical artifact dimension.
decisions.rationale: Prior turns (DEC-143 through DEC-158) completed formal specifications for input/output shapes and side effects/sensitivity but left auth as a gap. Without a formal spec, manifest consumers cannot interpret auth_hints correctly or make safe agent invocation decisions.

decisions.statement: Auth, side effects, and sensitivity are modeled as three separate governance dimensions rather than conflated into fewer fields. auth_hints answers 'what credentials are required?', side_effect_class answers 'does this mutate state?', and sensitivity_class answers 'what kind of data does this touch?'
decisions.rationale: Agent runtimes make three independent decisions: can I call this without confirmation (side effect), what audit/redaction rules apply (sensitivity), and do I have sufficient credentials (auth). Conflating these into fewer fields creates ambiguity and forces agent runtimes to over-approximate.

decisions.statement: V1 auth_hints remain identifier-based string arrays with no semantic parsing. V2 adds a structured auth_requirements object alongside the existing array for backward compatibility. Role extraction, scope mapping, tenant boundary detection, and least-privilege hints are all V2 goals.
decisions.rationale: Follows the same conservative-default pattern established for input_schema, output_schema, and sensitivity_class: ship the field, make the contract stable, defer inference accuracy to V2. Breaking changes to the artifact shape are worse than conservative V1 defaults.

decisions.statement: Planning signoff maintained as Approved: YES. All 4 required artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Status set to needs_human because planning_signoff gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to implementation phase.
decisions.rationale: All planning artifacts updated: SYSTEM_SPEC.md has complete auth specification, ROADMAP.md has M10 milestone, PM_SIGNOFF.md has Challenge 7, command-surface.md unchanged (no new commands). Gate requires human approval.

decisions.rationale: Launch narrative quality requires more than implementation accuracy. Early adopters need a concrete reason to inspect the manifest, and that reason is the visible review surface: provenance, side effects, sensitivity markers, and auth hints.

decisions.statement: Launch assets should define 'governed' in concrete product terms by explicitly pointing to `side_effect_class`, `sensitivity_class`, and `auth_hints` across planning docs and market-facing entrypoints.
decisions.rationale: Generic governance language is easy to skim past or misread as marketing abstraction. Naming the exact metadata keeps the launch narrative anchored in shipped product truth and improves qualification for the right first users.

summary: Formally specified examples and constraints as the 4th and 5th governance dimensions of the canonical artifact, completing all VISION.md line 55-59 dimensions. Added examples field to manifest Capability shape (V1: static placeholder, V2: test/JSDoc/OpenAPI inference), constraints field with 5 subfields (rate_limit, max_payload_bytes, required_headers, idempotent, cacheable; V1: all null/empty, V2: middleware detection). Updated compiled tool and MCP response shapes. ROADMAP M11 added. PM_SIGNOFF updated with Challenge 8.

decisions.statement: Challenged prior turns for leaving examples and constraints — two of the five VISION.md canonical artifact dimensions (line 59) — entirely unspecified in SYSTEM_SPEC.md. The examples field existed in compiled tools but was absent from the manifest shape. The constraints field did not exist anywhere in the pipeline.
decisions.rationale: VISION.md explicitly lists 'examples and constraints' alongside input/output shapes, side effects/sensitivity, and auth hints. All other dimensions had dedicated specification sections (DEC-143 through DEC-170). Leaving these two unspecified meant the canonical artifact contract was incomplete.

decisions.statement: Examples and constraints are modeled as two separate fields rather than conflated. Examples answer 'what does correct usage look like?' while constraints answer 'what operational limits apply?' Both follow the established conservative-default V1 pattern.
decisions.rationale: Each field drives distinct agent decisions: examples inform request construction, constraints inform throttling/retry/caching. Conflating them would create the same ambiguity that was avoided when separating side_effect_class from sensitivity_class (DEC-157).

decisions.statement: Constraints use a structured 5-field object (rate_limit, max_payload_bytes, required_headers, idempotent, cacheable) with all-null/empty defaults in V1, rather than a free-form string or deferred entirely to V2.
decisions.rationale: A structured shape establishes the contract now so downstream consumers can rely on field presence. Null values explicitly mean 'unknown' rather than 'no constraint'. This mirrors the sensitivity_class=unknown and input_schema=additionalProperties:true patterns from prior decisions.

decisions.statement: Planning signoff maintained as Approved: YES. All 4 required artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Status set to needs_human because the planning_signoff gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to implementation phase.
decisions.rationale: Gate requirements are met but human approval is required per the planning_signoff gate definition.

verification.evidence_summary: Website build passes with zero errors. All 4 planning artifacts exist and are internally consistent. SYSTEM_SPEC.md now includes formal Examples and Constraints specification sections with shapes, agent implications, V1 limitations, pipeline propagation, and V2 plans. Both fields are present in manifest Capability, compiled tool, and MCP response shapes. ROADMAP M11 milestone added with 7 checked items. PM_SIGNOFF updated with Challenge 8 and decision.

summary: Formally specified redaction and approval metadata — the last unspecified VISION.md canonical artifact dimension. Added redaction field (pii_fields, log_level, mask_in_traces, retention_days) with permissive V1 defaults to manifest, compiled tools, and MCP tools/call. Enriched approval metadata with approved_by and approved_at audit trail fields. Created dedicated specification sections with agent implications, V1 limitations, and V2 plans. The governance model is now six dimensions, completing all items listed in VISION.md lines 55-60.

decisions.statement: Challenged prior turns for leaving redaction and approval metadata — the last VISION.md canonical artifact dimension (line 60) — entirely unspecified in SYSTEM_SPEC.md. No redaction field existed anywhere in the pipeline. The approved/review_needed fields existed but had no formal specification section with agent implications, V1 limitations, or V2 plans.
decisions.rationale: VISION.md explicitly lists 'redaction and approval metadata' as a core canonical artifact dimension. Prior turns completed five of seven dimensions but left this gap. Downstream consumers (agent runtimes, logging systems, compliance tools) had no contract for data masking or approval audit trails.

decisions.statement: Redaction uses a 4-field structured object (pii_fields, log_level, mask_in_traces, retention_days) with permissive defaults in V1, rather than a free-form string or deferred entirely to V2.
decisions.rationale: Follows the established conservative-default V1 pattern from sensitivity_class and constraints. The structured shape establishes the contract for downstream consumers while permitting human authoring in V1 and automated inference in V2.

decisions.statement: Sensitivity and redaction are modeled as two separate fields rather than conflated. sensitivity_class answers 'what kind of data does this touch?' while redaction answers 'what operational masking/retention rules apply?'
decisions.rationale: Different teams may apply different redaction policies to the same sensitivity class. Keeping classification separate from operational response preserves policy flexibility. A capability can be confidential but have team-specific masking rules.

decisions.statement: Added approved_by (string|null) and approved_at (string|null) to the manifest capability shape to create an audit trail for the approval gate.
decisions.rationale: The approved boolean alone gates compilation but provides no traceability. Governance dashboards and compliance audits need to know who approved a capability and when. V1 defaults to null; V2 adds a tusq approve CLI that auto-populates these fields.

decisions.statement: Planning signoff maintained as Approved: YES. All 4 required artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Status set to needs_human because the planning_signoff gate requires human approval.
decisions.rationale: The governance model now covers all six dimensions listed in VISION.md lines 55-60. SYSTEM_SPEC, ROADMAP, PM_SIGNOFF, and command-surface.md are all present and updated. The only outstanding requirement is human approval to transition to implementation.

verification.evidence_summary: Website build passes with zero errors and zero broken links after SYSTEM_SPEC updates. All 4 planning artifacts exist and are internally consistent. The redaction field is added to manifest, compiled tool, and MCP tools/call shapes. Approval metadata enriched with approved_by and approved_at. The six-field governance model now covers all VISION.md canonical artifact dimensions.

summary: Specified version history and diffs — the seventh and final VISION.md canonical artifact dimension. Added manifest_version (integer counter), previous_manifest_hash (SHA-256 hash chain), and capability_digest (per-capability content hash) to manifest shape in SYSTEM_SPEC.md. Challenged prior turns for leaving this dimension entirely unspecified despite VISION.md lines 61, 218, and 274 explicitly requiring it. The governance model is now seven dimensions, completing all items in VISION.md lines 55-61.

decisions.statement: Challenged prior turns for leaving version history and diffs — the seventh and final VISION.md canonical artifact dimension (line 61) — entirely unspecified. No manifest versioning, content hashing, or diff mechanism existed anywhere in the pipeline despite VISION.md lines 61, 218, and 274 explicitly requiring them.
decisions.rationale: All 12 prior milestones covered dimensions 1-6 but left the last explicitly listed dimension as a gap. Without V1 fields establishing the contract, V2 diff tooling would require a breaking schema change.

decisions.statement: Version history uses three fields: manifest_version (integer counter at manifest root), previous_manifest_hash (SHA-256 hash chain at manifest root), and capability_digest (per-capability SHA-256 of content fields). V1 produces fields but ships no diff CLI or history file.
decisions.rationale: Follows the established conservative-default V1 pattern used for sensitivity_class, examples, constraints, and redaction. Fields establish the contract for V2 tooling without overclaiming V1 capabilities.

decisions.statement: Version history fields are manifest-only — they do not propagate to compiled tools (tusq-tools/*.json) or MCP responses (tools/list, tools/call). This matches the approval metadata pattern.
decisions.rationale: Version history tracks the evolution of the canonical artifact itself. Compiled tools are point-in-time snapshots of approved capabilities. MCP responses serve current state. Neither needs lineage information.

decisions.statement: capability_digest excludes approval/review metadata (approved, approved_by, approved_at, review_needed) and the digest itself. It includes all content fields (name, description, method, path, schemas, governance fields, provenance, confidence, domain).
decisions.rationale: The digest must answer 'did the capability itself change?' not 'did someone approve it?' Including approval state would cause false change signals on every human review action.

decisions.statement: Planning signoff maintained as Approved: YES. All 4 required artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Status set to needs_human because the planning_signoff gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to implementation phase.
decisions.rationale: The governance model now covers all seven dimensions listed in VISION.md lines 55-61. Planning artifacts are complete. Human approval is the only remaining gate requirement.

verification.evidence_summary: Website build exits 0 with no errors or broken links. CLI loads without error. All 4 planning artifacts exist and are internally consistent. SYSTEM_SPEC.md manifest root shape updated with manifest_version and previous_manifest_hash. Capability object updated with capability_digest. Version History and Diffs section added with full specification following established pattern. ROADMAP.md updated with M13 milestone. PM_SIGNOFF.md updated with Challenges 11-13 and signoff update.

summary: Added Framework Support Depth specification to SYSTEM_SPEC.md addressing vision goal 'support the most common framework stacks deeply within the first release' — formally specifies per-framework detection matrix, Node.js-only V1 rationale, detection limitations, and V2 expansion plan with plugin interface.

decisions.statement: Challenged prior turns for leaving framework support depth undocumented. V1 shipped three deep framework detectors but the planning artifacts never formally specified what 'deep' means per framework, why Node.js-only satisfies the vision requirement, or what the V2 expansion plan looks like.
decisions.rationale: VISION.md line 71 requires 'support the most common framework stacks deeply within the first release.' The implementation delivers depth but the planning artifacts didn't substantiate the claim. Without a formal spec, consumers of the planning docs cannot verify that V1 framework support actually meets the vision bar.

decisions.statement: V1 framework depth is defined as framework-specific extraction of 12 capabilities (method, path, handler, inline detection, middleware, auth hints, schema hints, domain, controller resolution, guard inheritance, deduplication, nearby code radius) rather than generic regex matching.
decisions.rationale: Each framework has fundamentally different routing conventions: Express uses app.verb() chains, Fastify supports both inline and route-object patterns, NestJS uses decorators with controller-level inheritance. A single generic pattern would miss framework-specific signals (NestJS guard inheritance, Fastify route blocks, Express middleware chains).

decisions.statement: Node.js-only V1 scope satisfies the vision's 'most common framework stacks' requirement because Express, Fastify, and NestJS cover the majority of Node.js backend APIs, and the vision explicitly prioritizes depth over breadth (line 114).
decisions.rationale: VISION line 114: 'Support fewer stacks, deeper. Node plus Python plus one or two serious backend frameworks, done correctly, beats thin support for twelve.' Python is mentioned as a V2 target alongside the Node.js stacks. V1 delivers the Node.js portion deeply; V2 adds Python via the plugin interface.

decisions.statement: V2 framework expansion will use the plugin interface (extractRoutes standard interface) rather than core code changes, enabling community framework adapters.
decisions.rationale: VISION line 130: 'Framework detectors... all live behind plugin interfaces. The core stays small. The ecosystem grows around it.' This is the architectural boundary that makes width sustainable.

decisions.statement: Planning signoff maintained as Approved: YES. All 4 required artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Status set to needs_human because the planning_signoff gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to implementation phase.
decisions.rationale: This is consistent with every prior PM turn conclusion. The planning artifacts are complete. Human approval is the only outstanding gate requirement.

Verified M14 milestone added to ROADMAP.md. Verified PM_SIGNOFF.md updated with Challenge 14 and Challenge 15.

summary: Turn reissued: ghost
Turn reissued: ghost
Turn reissued: ghost

Independently challenged dev turn_f38f05a4fef53bc4 on three grounds: Fastify inlineWithOptionsPattern handler-boundary quirk (resolved — correct output despite boundary), NestJS UseGuards false positive in auth_hints (resolved as known V1 heuristic noise), and Fastify route deduplication under mixed-pattern scan (resolved — no collision). node tests/smoke.mjs → exit 0. All 36 acceptance criteria PASS. Ship verdict SHIP stands. Awaiting human approval per qa_ship_verdict gate.

decisions.statement: Fastify inlineWithOptionsPattern captures handler expression as 'async function listOrders(' (boundary at listOrders() closing paren), but extractIdentifiers correctly isolates 'listOrders' after filtering blocked tokens; extraction is correct despite the regex boundary quirk.
decisions.rationale: isInline check requires function\s*\( with no name between function and (, so named async functions are treated as non-inline; the last token in the filtered list is the correct handler name.

decisions.statement: NestJS auth_hints includes UseGuards (the decorator function) alongside AuthGuard and AdminGuard as a known V1 heuristic false positive. This is acceptable noise under the regex-based scanner model defined by DEC-214; a V2 AST-based scanner would exclude decorator names from the hint set.
decisions.rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC defines auth_hints as regex-extracted identifiers in auth context. UseGuards appears in that context. The heuristic model accepts decorator-name noise at V1 scope.

decisions.statement: Ship verdict stands as SHIP. All 36 acceptance criteria PASS. Status remains needs_human because qa_ship_verdict gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to launch phase.
decisions.rationale: No blocking issues found. Both independent challenges raised in this turn resolved as non-blocking. Smoke tests independently verified exit 0 on 2026-04-20.

summary: Challenged all prior turns for focusing on governance metadata while leaving first-pass manifest output unusable for LLM consumers. Specified four concrete V1 improvements — path parameter extraction, smart domain inference, rich capability descriptions, honest confidence penalty — that transform manifests from structurally present but functionally empty to actionable with targeted review. Updated SYSTEM_SPEC.md, ROADMAP.md (M15), and PM_SIGNOFF.md (Challenge 16).

decisions.statement: Challenged ALL prior turns (DEC-143 through DEC-223) for addressing governance metadata dimensions while leaving the core extraction output — the fields an LLM actually reads to understand a tool — functionally useless on first pass. VISION.md lines 72-73 explicitly require usable first-pass manifests and treat manual authoring as engine failure.
decisions.rationale: Prior turns added sensitivity_class, auth_hints, examples, constraints, redaction, versioning, and framework depth specifications. All are necessary governance metadata. But a manifest where every capability description says 'GET /users capability in users domain', every input_schema is { type: object, additionalProperties: true }, and domain inference produces 'api' instead of 'users' requires complete manual rewrite before an LLM can use it. The governance decorations are meaningless if the base extraction is unusable.

decisions.statement: Path parameters (:id, {id}) extracted from route paths into input_schema.properties as required string fields. Uses regex on information already in the route path — no AST parsing needed.
decisions.rationale: An LLM receiving { type: 'object', additionalProperties: true } for a tool at /users/:id has no idea it needs to provide an 'id' argument. Extracting the parameter name from the path and adding it as a required property is the single highest-impact improvement for first-pass usability.

decisions.statement: Domain inference skips well-known API prefixes (api, v1-v5, rest, graphql, internal, public, external) before selecting the first meaningful path segment.
decisions.rationale: The current inferDomain() takes the literal first segment, so /api/v1/users produces domain 'api'. This cascades into wrong capability names and meaningless grouping. Skipping prefixes is a 10-line change that fixes domain accuracy for the majority of real-world route paths.

decisions.statement: Capability descriptions use a rich template (verb + noun + qualifier + side_effect + auth_context + handler) instead of the static 'METHOD /path capability in domain domain' string.
decisions.rationale: The description field is what an LLM reads to decide whether a tool is relevant. 'Retrieve user by id — read-only, requires requireAuth (handler: getUser)' provides semantic context. 'GET /api/v1/users/:id capability in api domain' provides none. All template fields are already computed before describeCapability() runs.

decisions.statement: Confidence scoring applies a -0.10 penalty when no schema hint is detected, pushing schema-less routes with named handlers and auth below the review_needed threshold of 0.8.
decisions.rationale: A route scoring 0.86 with additionalProperties: true and no real schema misleads reviewers into thinking the engine understood the route well. The penalty makes the manifest honest: routes where the engine could not extract parameter structure are flagged for review.

decisions.statement: Planning signoff maintained as Approved: YES. All 4 required artifacts are complete and internally consistent. Status set to needs_human because the planning_signoff gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to implementation phase.
decisions.rationale: M15 is fully specified in SYSTEM_SPEC.md and ROADMAP.md. The specifications use only information the scanner already computes — no new parsing infrastructure needed. Human approval is required by the gate definition.

verification.evidence_summary: Verified all three planning artifacts contain the new first-pass usability content: SYSTEM_SPEC.md has the full specification section (14 occurrences of related terms), ROADMAP.md has M15 milestone with 6 unchecked items, PM_SIGNOFF.md has Challenge 16 with decision rationale. Cross-references between documents are consistent (SYSTEM_SPEC references from confidence scoring table and domain field description both point to the new section).

summary: Challenged prior QA turn as procedural-only; independently re-verified product truth on HEAD 273a23b; all four launch artifacts remain aligned with shipped v0.1.0; launch_ready gate returned to human for approval.

decisions.statement: Challenged the last accepted turn (qa turn_a325dad1e69c9d1e, DEC-269–DEC-271) as a procedural verification pass: it added no new acceptance criteria and made no source/test/doc changes. It correctly re-ran smoke and CLI surface checks, but a QA-phase re-verification does not by itself advance the launch gate — launch copy still needs an independent product-truth pass against current HEAD before the launch_ready gate is returned to the human.
decisions.rationale: Protocol rule 1 requires explicit challenge of the previous turn. The prior QA turn was valid work but cannot substitute for a launch-phase alignment check, which is this turn's mandate.

decisions.statement: Launch artifacts remain aligned with shipped v0.1.0 product truth on HEAD 273a23b as of 2026-04-21. MESSAGING.md (130 lines) leads with audience filter, operator problem, proof sequence, and V1 boundary before governance-metadata inventory. LAUNCH_PLAN.md (102 lines) enforces the four-question first-screen order (who / problem / proof / boundary). CONTENT_CALENDAR.md (50 lines) repeats the repo-local CTA and the proof-path one-liner. ANNOUNCEMENT.md (138 lines) names the release as tusq.dev v0.1.0 and uses the repo-local `git clone` trial path (not `npm install -g`), matching package.json private:true.
decisions.rationale: Sharpening positioning without drifting from product truth requires confirming the artifacts still match what shipped. The CLI surface, framework scope, approval trail fields, redaction, examples/constraints, and describe-only MCP claims in every artifact match current HEAD behavior. No edits needed this turn — edits without a product-truth delta would be churn.

decisions.statement: launch_ready gate is returned to human for approval rather than self-declared by this agent PM. All four required artifacts exist with concrete, internally consistent content; verification evidence is attached (smoke exit 0, help exit 0, package identity tusq@0.1.0 private:true, origin matches ANNOUNCEMENT.md); the gate explicitly requires human approval before run completion.
decisions.rationale: Protocol rule 5 and the launch_ready gate definition both require human approval at this juncture. Self-declaring run_completion_request:true would violate the gate contract regardless of artifact completeness.

decisions.rationale: Honest challenge of inherited state: the prior PM evidence was accurate when produced but is now stale relative to HEAD e838d9f. A dev turn that inherits 'all complete' assertions without checking the live ROADMAP would miss new planned scope.

decisions.statement: M16 (Manifest Diff and Review Queue) is PM-selected and planning-complete on HEAD e838d9f: NEXT_INCREMENT.md defines scope; ROADMAP.md lists 8 implementation tasks; SYSTEM_SPEC.md carries the diff command spec; command-surface.md documents the new surface; acceptance-matrix.md defines REQ-039–REQ-044 (all status PLANNED). summary: Challenged prior product_marketing turn and updated all four launch artifacts to reflect the shipped M18 `tusq approve` CLI on HEAD ba97410; verified npm test passes with both smoke and eval suites.

decisions.statement: Challenged the prior accepted product_marketing turn (turn_091e4cb55d4f3708, HEAD 5e38bc2) rather than rubber-stamping it. That turn pre-dated M18 (commit f0e42d4, 'Add governed manifest approval CLI') and left launch copy describing approval as a manifest hand-edit even though `tusq approve` has now shipped as a first-class CLI command with `--all`, `--reviewer`, `--manifest`, `--dry-run`, `--json`, and `--verbose` flags. Performed fresh on-disk audit on HEAD ba97410.
decisions.rationale: Accepting stale launch artifacts would publish a first-screen workflow that contradicts the shipped product surface. The buyer would hit a 10-command CLI with a governed approval verb but read launch copy that tells them to edit JSON. That is a launch-day credibility failure, exactly the failure mode the product_marketing mandate exists to prevent.

decisions.statement: Updated MESSAGING.md, LAUNCH_PLAN.md, ANNOUNCEMENT.md, and CONTENT_CALENDAR.md to call `tusq approve` by name in every workflow example, command inventory, and proof-path line. MESSAGING.md Product Truth now lists the full 10-command surface (init, scan, manifest, compile, serve, review, approve, diff, version, help) and the exact `tusq approve` flag set. LAUNCH_PLAN.md terminal workflow example replaces 'edit approvals' with `tusq approve --all --reviewer you@example.com`. ANNOUNCEMENT.md step 4, bash block, how-to-try-it section, and community-post command list all now reference `tusq approve`. CONTENT_CALENDAR.md launch-day post and Day 1-2 proof-path post now call the approve CLI as the governed operator action.
decisions.rationale: M18 shipped governance-as-a-verb: approval is now a recorded CLI action with reviewer identity and timestamp, not an implicit manifest edit. Launch copy must match so 'governed' remains a concrete, demonstrable claim instead of slogan-level language. This is the core narrative differentiator against prompt wrappers, and burying it behind 'edit the manifest' would waste the proof.

decisions.statement: Status is needs_human because the launch_ready gate explicitly requires human approval before run completion. The four launch_ready artifacts are now complete, internally consistent, and aligned with the shipped 10-command CLI surface on HEAD ba97410. npm test exits 0 with both smoke tests and the eval regression harness passing. All upstream gates (planning_signoff, implementation_complete, qa_ship_verdict) are passed.
decisions.rationale: An agent product_marketing role cannot self-declare launch_ready gate satisfaction; the gate is explicitly human-approval-gated. proposed_next_role is 'human' so the orchestrator routes to the reviewer for launch_ready sign-off, after which the launch phase can complete.

summary: Challenged prior turn, detected regression in .planning/command-surface.md (stripped from 203 lines to a 17-line empty template on HEAD 53609d5), restored the accepted content, and re-verified shipped product boundary on HEAD 53609d5.

decisions.statement: Challenged the prior accepted turn (turn_f15847c7264a7e6c, role=product_marketing, phase=launch, HEAD 53609d5) rather than rubber-stamping it. Refused to inherit parent-run state or prior-turn evidence; performed fresh PM-authored on-disk verification on HEAD 53609d5 as of 2026-04-22.
decisions.rationale: Protocol requires explicit challenge of prior work; prior-turn checkpoint commit touched .planning/command-surface.md with a -196/+10 diff that warranted direct inspection rather than inherited trust.

decisions.statement: Identified and fixed a regression on a required planning artifact I own: .planning/command-surface.md was stripped from its 203-line accepted state on HEAD 4ba95f3 to a 17-line empty table template on HEAD 53609d5 by a product_marketing launch-phase checkpoint. Restored the file to its accepted 203-line content covering the shipped tusq CLI surface (including tusq approve and tusq diff subcommands with flags and failure UX) plus the Docusaurus website build surface.
decisions.rationale: The planning_signoff gate declares .planning/command-surface.md as a required artifact with section_check semantics and pm ownership. An empty template does not satisfy section_check. A launch-phase role should not have rewritten a planning-phase artifact to empty; as planning-phase owner with authoritative write authority, restoring the accepted content is the correct remedy.

decisions.statement: All four required planning artifacts are now structurally complete and internally consistent on HEAD 53609d5 (dirty) as of 2026-04-22: PM_SIGNOFF.md (229 lines, 'Approved: YES' on line 3), ROADMAP.md (166 lines, 110 checked / 0 open covering M1-M18), SYSTEM_SPEC.md (1,545 lines covering the 7 VISION canonical artifact dimensions + framework depth + M15 first-pass usability + M16 diff spec + M18 approval CLI), command-surface.md (203 lines, restored; includes the 10-command surface and full tusq approve + tusq diff flag tables and failure UX).
decisions.rationale: The planning_signoff gate requires each of these artifacts to exist and be complete. Structural completeness is necessary but not sufficient — content must also match shipped product truth, verified below.

decisions.statement: Shipped product behavior matches the planning boundary on HEAD 53609d5 (dirty): npm test exits 0 with 'Smoke tests passed' and 'Eval regression harness passed (2 scenarios)'; bin/tusq.js help enumerates the shipped 10-command surface (init, scan, manifest, compile, serve, review, approve, diff, version, help); bin/tusq.js approve --help emits the planned flag set ([capability-name] [--all] [--reviewer <id>] [--manifest <path>] [--dry-run] [--json] [--verbose]); bin/tusq.js diff --help emits the planned flag set ([--from <path>] [--to <path>] [--json] [--review-queue] [--fail-on-unapproved-changes] [--verbose]).
decisions.rationale: Direct runtime verification is required to ensure the planning artifacts describe the system as it actually behaves, not as it was intended to behave on a prior commit.

decisions.statement: Planning signoff remains Approved: YES in PM_SIGNOFF.md. Status is needs_human because the planning_signoff exit gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to the implementation phase; an agent PM cannot self-declare gate satisfaction regardless of artifact completeness. proposed_next_role is 'human' so the orchestrator routes to the reviewer, not to dev.
decisions.rationale: The exit gate definition includes 'Requires human approval' — the orchestrator's contract is clear that the PM cannot self-approve the phase transition.

objections.statement: The prior launch-phase product_marketing checkpoint (turn_f15847c7264a7e6c, HEAD 53609d5) rewrote a planning-phase artifact (.planning/command-surface.md) down to an empty template, effectively erasing ~196 lines of accepted CLI command surface documentation owned by the pm role. Launch-phase roles should not strip planning-phase workflow artifacts. Recommend the orchestrator enforce that checkpoint commits from a given phase cannot net-delete artifact sections owned by earlier phases.

summary: Challenged prior launch artifacts for omitting shipped M19 tusq docs command; updated MESSAGING, LAUNCH_PLAN, CONTENT_CALENDAR, and ANNOUNCEMENT to reflect the 11-command surface and add tusq docs as the offline Markdown review artifact.

decisions.statement: Challenged prior product_marketing turn turn_f15847c7264a7e6c (HEAD 53609d5) rather than rubber-stamping its launch copy. Independent audit on HEAD 8f7560a found a regression: all four launch_ready gate artifacts still described a 10-command CLI surface and omitted tusq docs, even though M19 (commits 99f77ac → f735611) shipped tusq docs as a first-class subcommand with --manifest, --out, and --verbose flags and deterministic Markdown output, and QA already accepted REQ-054–REQ-057 for that behavior.
decisions.rationale: Launch copy must match shipped product truth. Publishing an announcement that names only 10 commands after an 11th has shipped and been QA-accepted would mis-state the product surface on launch day and undermine every subsequent 'governed, inspectable' claim.

decisions.statement: Updated MESSAGING.md (Launch Correction, proof sentence, Product Truth 11-command list, tusq docs flag line, Claims We Can Defend), LAUNCH_PLAN.md (Challenge To Prior Framing, must-publish boundary line, adoption-artifact workflow example), CONTENT_CALENDAR.md (launch-day workflow post callout, Day 3-4 tusq docs share post), and ANNOUNCEMENT.md (step 8, proof-points paragraph, bash block, how-to-try-it, community-post command list and V1 scope) to reference tusq docs by name and add the offline Markdown review-packet framing.
decisions.rationale: tusq docs is the shareable artifact that closes the gap between CLI operators and non-CLI reviewers. Framing it as an offline, deterministic, checked-in review packet reinforces the 'governed, inspectable' narrative and gives reviewers an artifact they can audit without running the CLI themselves.

decisions.statement: Status is needs_human because the launch_ready gate explicitly requires human approval before run completion. The four launch_ready artifacts are now complete, internally consistent, and aligned with the shipped 11-command CLI surface on HEAD 8f7560a (workspace dirty). All upstream gates (planning_signoff, implementation_complete, qa_ship_verdict) are passed. proposed_next_role is 'human' so the orchestrator routes to the reviewer, not back to another agent role.
decisions.rationale: An agent PM cannot self-declare gate satisfaction regardless of artifact completeness. Run completion must be authorized by a human reviewer. run_completion_request is set to true to signal intent, but status stays needs_human so the orchestrator holds the transition for human approval.

decisions.statement: Challenged the prior accepted product_marketing turn turn_c655ad3f7af911d3 (HEAD 8ed5ec6) rather than rubber-stamping it. Refused to inherit prior-turn evidence; performed fresh product_marketing-authored on-disk audit of every launch_ready gate artifact and independently re-ran the full CLI verification surface on current HEAD 8ed5ec6 as of 2026-04-22.
decisions.rationale: Protocol Rule 1 requires every turn to challenge the previous turn explicitly. The prior turn correctly restored tusq docs coverage across the four launch_ready artifacts, but launch-phase regressions have recurred in this run (prior launch turn 53609d5 had stripped command-surface.md; earlier launch turns had omitted tusq approve and tusq diff). Confirming via independent verification rather than assuming the prior turn's claims is the only way to avoid quiet drift inside the launch phase.

decisions.statement: All four launch_ready gate artifacts are complete, internally consistent, and aligned with the shipped 11-command CLI surface on HEAD 8ed5ec6. Independent line-level verification: MESSAGING.md line 99 enumerates the 11-command surface (init, scan, manifest, compile, serve, review, docs, approve, diff, version, help); line 100 reproduces the exact tusq approve flag set; line 101 names tusq diff with --review-queue and --fail-on-unapproved-changes; line 102 reproduces the exact tusq docs flag set [--manifest <path>] [--out <path>] [--verbose]. LAUNCH_PLAN.md line 9 explicitly calls out the M19 tusq docs regression correction; line 54 adds the tusq docs --out adoption-artifact workflow example. CONTENT_CALENDAR.md line 9 adds the launch-day tusq docs callout; lines 37 adds the Day 3-4 tusq docs share post. ANNOUNCEMENT.md step 8 (line 28), proof-points paragraph (line 32), bash block (lines 65-66), how-to-try-it bullet (line 91), and community-post command list (line 131) plus V1 scope (line 140) all reference tusq docs. Total tusq docs mentions: MESSAGING 4, LAUNCH_PLAN 3, CONTENT_CALENDAR 2, ANNOUNCEMENT 5.
decisions.rationale: Every gate artifact's reference to the shipped CLI surface must match what the running binary actually exposes. Grep-level and read-level audits of all four files confirm the prior turn's content corrections survive on HEAD 8ed5ec6 without regression, and the CLI surface and flag strings match the running binary exactly.

decisions.statement: Propose run completion. All four launch_ready gate artifacts exist, are internally consistent with the shipped 11-command CLI surface on HEAD 8ed5ec6, and correctly scope M19 (tusq docs offline Markdown capability documentation) into every operator-facing workflow example and command inventory. Upstream gates (planning_signoff, implementation_complete, qa_ship_verdict) are all passed. npm test exits 0 with both smoke tests and the eval regression harness passing. No remaining workflow-kit obligations are open for the product_marketing role. Status is needs_human because the launch_ready gate explicitly requires human approval before run completion; routing to human for launch_ready gate approval with run_completion_request=true.
decisions.rationale: The launch_ready gate is the final gate in the run. All four required gate artifacts are complete and verified, all upstream gates are passed, and shipped product behavior matches launch copy. An agent product_marketing role cannot self-declare gate satisfaction; the orchestrator must route to human for the terminal gate approval. Setting proposed_next_role='human' and run_completion_request=true with phase_transition_request=null is the correct terminal signal for a launch phase role.

summary: Derived M20 (opt-in local execution-policy scaffold for MCP serve) planning artifacts from the approved intake intent and VISION.md; extended ROADMAP.md, SYSTEM_SPEC.md, command-surface.md, and PM_SIGNOFF.md without claiming any implementation work shipped.

decisions.statement: Challenged the prior commit 71c4abc 'Start AgentXchain M20 planning recovery' rather than inheriting it. That commit only unarchived the M20 intent and rewrote intake/state.json — it did not extend any of the four PM-owned planning artifacts. ROADMAP.md stopped at M19, SYSTEM_SPEC.md had no execution-policy section, command-surface.md had no tusq serve --policy surface, and PM_SIGNOFF had no M20 challenge. The planning_signoff gate cannot pass for an M20-scoped run while the planning artifacts describe only M1–M19.
decisions.rationale: Protocol Rule 1 requires an explicit challenge; rubber-stamping the orchestration commit would leave the gate incoherent with the approved intake intent.

decisions.statement: M20 scope is exactly one rung of the VISION safe-execution-wrapper ladder: opt-in dry-run argument validation plus auditable plan emission on the MCP tools/call path. No tusq execute, no confirm/approve ladders on serve, no live API execution, no outbound I/O to the target product under any policy mode. Explicitly rejected three alternatives: (A) a hosted sandbox, (B) a tusq execute --confirm CLI, (C) full JSON Schema draft-07 validation with format/enum/oneOf.
decisions.rationale: The intake acceptance contract requires 'scope stays local/offline and does not perform live product API execution.' Collapsing dry-run with confirm or execute would violate that contract and the VISION.md safe-execution-wrapper ladder.

decisions.statement: Opt-in is carried by a new governance artifact .tusq/execution-policy.json (schema_version, mode: describe-only | dry-run, optional allowed_capabilities, reviewer, approved_at) and a single new CLI flag tusq serve --policy <path>. Without --policy, tusq serve behavior is byte-for-byte identical to V1 describe-only. The approval gate is preserved as an invariant: only approved: true capabilities appear in tools/list, and allowed_capabilities is a strict subset filter on top of approval, never a replacement.
decisions.rationale: A governance artifact is reviewable the same way a manifest is reviewable; this keeps operator adoption explicit rather than implicit and gives the launch phase something concrete to document as a reviewable file.

decisions.statement: The dry-run response from tools/call under mode: dry-run ships with an explicit executed: false marker, a policy echo block (mode, reviewer, approved_at), and a dry_run_plan object with fields method, path, path_params, query, body, headers, auth_context, side_effect_class, sensitivity_class, redaction, plan_hash, and evaluated_at. plan_hash is a SHA-256 of a canonical JSON serialization of {method, path, path_params, query, body, headers} and deliberately excludes evaluated_at, policy echoes, and executed so identical validated inputs produce identical hashes.
decisions.rationale: Challenge 19 — plan_hash is the first replay primitive on the serve path. Shipping it now prevents a breaking shape change later when V2 adds confirm/replay/plan-diff tooling.

decisions.statement: Argument validation depth for V1.1 is intentionally narrow: required field enforcement, primitive type checks (string/number/integer/boolean), and additionalProperties: false rejection for schemas that declare it. No format, enum, oneOf, or regex validation in V1.1. Validation errors are surfaced as JSON-RPC -32602 with data.validation_errors: [{path, reason}].
decisions.rationale: Narrow validation covers the argument failure modes an LLM actually produces (missing required, wrong type, made-up property) without committing to an external JSON Schema validator dependency or a reimplementation. Deeper validation is deferred to V2.

decisions.statement: ROADMAP.md gained an M20 milestone with 10 unchecked items and two new Key Risk rows (dry-run plan mistaken for live execution; execution policy drift between manifest governance and serve). No item is claimed checked. This PM turn is deriving work, not verifying shipped behavior.
decisions.rationale: Honest planning means not checking boxes the PM has not personally verified. Prior PM precedent (DEC-192: 'Leave M13 unchecked — the implementation was done in a prior run, but this PM turn is not claiming to have verified the implementation.').

decisions.statement: Planning artifacts now enumerate the docs and tests required before implementation per the intake acceptance contract: policy-off smoke, policy-on dry-run success smoke, validation-failure smoke, plan_hash determinism, eval regression for approval-gate invariance, CLI reference docs update, new execution-policy.md page, MCP server docs dry-run section, and README --policy entry. Dev accountability for implementation; QA accountability for independent re-verification as REQ-058–REQ-063 (QA owns the final REQ numbering).
decisions.rationale: The intake charter explicitly required 'planning updates identify docs/tests needed before implementation'; a dev turn cannot sign off implementation_complete without covering these six surfaces.

decisions.statement: Status is needs_human and proposed_next_role is 'human' because the planning_signoff exit gate explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to the implementation phase; an agent PM cannot self-declare gate satisfaction regardless of artifact completeness. phase_transition_request is null.
decisions.rationale: The gate spec in this turn's assignment ('Requires human approval') is a hard requirement. Prior PM turns (DEC-435, DEC-440) followed the same routing rule.

decisions.statement: Challenged the prior accepted turn (turn_7c7d7f3f7b5bbc55, HEAD d3589c0, checkpoint commit of the M20 planning derivation) rather than rubber-stamping it. Refused to inherit the prior turn's evidence claim; performed fresh PM-authored on-disk verification of every required planning artifact and independently re-ran the shipped CLI surface against the planning boundary on 2026-04-22.
decisions.rationale: Protocol rule 1 requires explicit challenge of the previous turn. The prior turn's claim that all four planning artifacts were extended for M20 is load-bearing for the planning_signoff gate, so it must be re-verified rather than trusted. Independent verification confirmed the claim but also surfaced a minor Challenge-ordering issue that rubber-stamping would have hidden.

decisions.statement: All four required planning artifacts are structurally complete for M20 on HEAD d3589c0 (workspace dirty) as of 2026-04-22. Line counts: PM_SIGNOFF.md 277, ROADMAP.md 188, SYSTEM_SPEC.md 1708, command-surface.md 264. Every artifact references M20 with substantive content (PM_SIGNOFF: 6 M20 mentions including Challenges 18-20 and the 2026-04-22 update note; ROADMAP: 2 mentions anchoring the M20 milestone section and a new Key Risk row; SYSTEM_SPEC: 5 mentions including the full M20 section at line 1539, error code table at line 1639, approval-gate invariants at line 1647, and local-only invariant at line 1708; command-surface: 5 mentions including the M20 Product CLI Surface at line 56, serve options table, dry-run response table, and dry-run failure UX table).
decisions.rationale: The planning_signoff gate needs all four files to exist and carry M20-scoped content. Grep confirms every file references M20, wc confirms substantive page counts, and targeted reads confirm the content matches the DEC-462 through DEC-467 decisions recorded by the prior turn (policy file shape, serve --policy flag, dry_run_plan fields, plan_hash SHA-256 rule, narrow V1.1 validation, preserved approval gate, docs-and-tests-required list).

decisions.statement: Shipped product behavior matches the planning boundary. npm test exits 0 with both 'Smoke tests passed' and 'Eval regression harness passed (2 scenarios)'. bin/tusq.js help still enumerates the shipped 11-command surface (init, scan, manifest, compile, serve, review, docs, approve, diff, version, help). bin/tusq.js serve --help still prints 'Usage: tusq serve [--port <n>] [--verbose]' — no --policy flag is present, correctly reflecting that M20 is planned-not-shipped work owned by the dev role in the next phase.
decisions.rationale: Independent verification of the planning boundary: planning artifacts describe M20 as implementation-ready planned work, and the running binary confirms no M20 behavior has leaked into shipped code. This is the invariant that makes an unchecked M20 milestone in ROADMAP.md accurate rather than stale.

decisions.statement: Non-blocking cosmetic observation: Challenges in PM_SIGNOFF.md are not in numeric order around the M20 block (sequence runs Challenge 15 → Challenge 18 → Challenge 19 → Challenge 20 → Challenge 17). This does not affect gate satisfaction because every referenced challenge exists and carries its intended content; the gate has no ordering requirement. Leaving the ordering as-is rather than rewriting the file to preserve review locality of the M20 challenges together at the tail of the challenges section.
decisions.rationale: Raising this observation here so the record is honest rather than pretending the file is pristine. Rewriting the file to reorder challenges would churn history and risk introducing content drift for a purely cosmetic concern. The planning_signoff gate checklist does not require sequential challenge numbering.

Setting phase_transition_request would usurp routing authority reserved for the human reviewer. proposed_next_role=human routes the orchestrator to the reviewer, not to the dev role.

summary: Implemented M20 opt-in local execution policy scaffold: --policy flag on tusq serve, dry-run argument validation, plan_hash computation, policy startup failure UX, smoke and eval regression tests for REQ-058 through REQ-063, and execution-policy.md website doc.

decisions.statement: Challenged prior accepted PM turn (turn_33545c65a9905eff) — it performed planning artifact verification only and cannot satisfy the implementation_complete gate. Independent dev-authored implementation and fresh runtime verification performed on HEAD d3589c0.
decisions.rationale: Protocol rule 1 requires explicitly challenging the previous turn rather than rubber-stamping it. The PM turn produced no code changes; implementation is the dev role's accountability.

decisions.statement: Added method and path to compiled tool artifacts in cmdCompile. The dry-run plan builder reads these fields from the compiled tool, matching the spec's 'copied from the compiled tool' language. Old compiled tools without these fields default to null/GET in the plan.
decisions.rationale: The SYSTEM_SPEC M20 section states 'dry_run_plan.method: HTTP method copied from the compiled tool'. The compile step is the correct place to persist route metadata into the tool artifact rather than re-reading the manifest at serve time.

decisions.statement: Implemented loadAndValidatePolicy with strict schema_version='1.0' enforcement, mode whitelist (describe-only|dry-run), and allowed_capabilities type check. All failures exit 1 with actionable messages matching the command-surface.md failure UX table verbatim.
decisions.rationale: The policy file is a governance artifact. Startup validation is the right time to fail fast — not at first RPC call — so the operator sees the error synchronously in their terminal session.

decisions.statement: plan_hash is SHA-256 over canonical (key-sorted) JSON of {body, headers, method, path, path_params, query}, using the existing sortKeysDeep utility already in cli.js. The evaluated_at and policy echo fields are intentionally excluded to ensure identical inputs produce identical hashes.
decisions.rationale: The SYSTEM_SPEC M20 section specifies: 'plan_hash is a SHA-256 over a canonical JSON serialization of {method, path, path_params, query, body, headers}. Identical inputs produce identical plans.' sortKeysDeep already existed in cli.js and provides the determinism guarantee.

decisions.statement: V1.1 argument validation covers: required field enforcement, primitive type checks (string/number/integer/boolean), and additionalProperties:false rejection. Object, array, and null typed parameters are not strictly validated per the spec's explicit V1.1 limitation table.
decisions.rationale: The SYSTEM_SPEC M20 section explicitly limits V1.1 validation depth and defers format/enum/oneOf/regex to V2. Implementing only what the spec requires avoids over-shipping.

decisions.statement: Added 4 new eval scenarios to eval-regression.mjs (up from 2): policy-dry-run-plan-shape and policy-dry-run-approval-gate. These required adding spawn and http imports to the eval harness plus a new runPolicyEvalScenario function that starts a live server, makes RPC assertions, then stops it. npm test exits 0 with 'Eval regression harness passed (4 scenarios)'.
decisions.rationale: The planning spec required eval scenarios in governed-cli-scenarios.json asserting dry-run plan shape and approval gate invariance under policy. These require HTTP server interaction, so the eval harness needed async server infrastructure analogous to smoke.mjs.

decisions.statement: No live API execution was added under any code path. The M20 local-only invariant (SYSTEM_SPEC line 1708) is satisfied: executed:false is always present in every dry-run response, and the plan builder does not make outbound HTTP, DB, or socket I/O.
decisions.rationale: This is the hard invariant for M20. Dry-run = in-process plan construction only. Live execution is explicitly deferred past V1.1.

verification.evidence_summary: serve --help prints 'Usage: tusq serve [--port <n>] [--policy <path>] [--verbose]'. help enumerates the unchanged 11-command surface. npm test exits 0 with 'Smoke tests passed' and 'Eval regression harness passed (4 scenarios)'. Smoke tests cover REQ-058 (policy startup failures: missing file, bad JSON, unsupported version, unknown mode), REQ-059 (describe-only policy is a no-op), REQ-060 (dry-run plan with path param substitution, executed:false, policy echo), REQ-061 (plan_hash determinism), REQ-062 (validation failure with data.validation_errors), REQ-063 (allowed_capabilities filter). Eval harness runs 4 scenarios: express-governed-workflow, manifest-diff-review-queue, policy-dry-run-plan-shape, policy-dry-run-approval-gate.

decisions.rationale: The dev turn's smoke tests explicitly cover these 6 behaviors but no acceptance criteria existed for them. QA must formally register acceptance criteria before signing off on any increment.

decisions.rationale: RELEASE_NOTES must accurately describe all shipped behavior. The M20 dry-run policy is a user-facing capability change that requires documentation in the release notes.

summary: Challenged prior QA turn: M20 (REQ-058–REQ-063) shipped and was SHIP-verdicted, but all four launch_ready artifacts still described M1–M19 only. Updated MESSAGING, LAUNCH_PLAN, CONTENT_CALENDAR, and ANNOUNCEMENT to cover tusq serve --policy, .tusq/execution-policy.json, opt-in dry-run, dry_run_plan shape, plan_hash determinism, and the preserved approval-gate and local-only invariants. npm test passes (4 eval scenarios). Routing to human for launch_ready gate approval.

decisions.statement: Challenged the prior accepted turn (turn_2aae9109760b8f5f, role=qa, HEAD ab55837) rather than rubber-stamping its SHIP verdict. Independent on-disk audit of the four launch_ready artifacts on HEAD ab55837 found that none of them described M20 shipped behavior: MESSAGING.md, LAUNCH_PLAN.md, CONTENT_CALENDAR.md, and ANNOUNCEMENT.md all described a single describe-only tools/call response shape, did not mention tusq serve --policy, did not name .tusq/execution-policy.json, did not describe the dry_run_plan response, and did not mention plan_hash — even though M20 was merged, REQ-058–REQ-063 were accepted, and ship-verdict stood as SHIP.
decisions.rationale: This is the same regression pattern previously caught at M16 (tusq diff), M18 (tusq approve), and M19 (tusq docs): shipped dev/QA work advances, but launch_ready artifacts lag. Accepting the QA turn without repairing launch copy would push the launch narrative out of alignment with the shipped CLI surface and violate protocol rule 1 (challenge the previous turn explicitly).

decisions.statement: Updated .planning/MESSAGING.md to add: (a) an M20 Launch Correction bullet naming the regression, (b) an updated proof sentence that distinguishes describe-only (default) from opt-in dry-run, (c) Product Truth lines for `tusq serve --policy`, the `.tusq/execution-policy.json` fields, the dry_run_plan response shape including plan_hash and evaluated_at, and the explicit invariant that --policy never bypasses the approval gate, (d) a Claims We Can Defend bullet for the opt-in dry-run capability, and (e) three new Claims We Must Not Make bullets clarifying that dry-run is not live execution, --policy is not 'live execution with a safety net', and --policy is never the default.
decisions.rationale: Every shipped M20 mechanism needs a launch-copy home that matches spec language exactly. Using the command-surface.md M20 section as the source ensures the messaging reads against the same facts QA verified.

decisions.statement: Updated .planning/LAUNCH_PLAN.md to add: (a) an M20 Challenge To Prior Framing bullet, (b) a must-publish-at-launch line adding opt-in dry-run plan emission to the verified product boundary, (c) an optional dry-run workflow example that authors an execution-policy and starts tusq serve --policy, and (d) an Expectation-mismatch-on-MCP risk update clarifying that dry-run still never executes and must not be described as 'live execution with a safety net'.
decisions.rationale: Launch Plan is the working guide that downstream assets inherit. If the plan still frames tools/call as a single response shape, downstream artifacts regress to that framing in future turns.

decisions.statement: Updated .planning/CONTENT_CALENDAR.md to add: (a) a launch-day callout that tusq serve --policy is available as an opt-in dry-run option, (b) a revised launch-day proof-path line that includes the dry-run option, and (c) a Day 3-4 'opt-in dry-run, not live execution' post focused on M20, its dry_run_plan response shape, plan_hash determinism, the preserved approval gate, and the fact that --policy is never the default.
decisions.rationale: Social and community posts need an explicit M20 slot or the launch calendar will omit the feature from the post-launch narrative. The 'opt-in dry-run, not live execution' framing matches the Claims We Must Not Make discipline established in MESSAGING.md.

decisions.statement: Updated .planning/ANNOUNCEMENT.md to add: (a) a new step 9 in the product-summary workflow covering tusq serve --policy, (b) a revised proof-points paragraph naming the opt-in dry-run response shape, (c) a bash-block line showing tusq serve --policy .tusq/execution-policy.json in the terminal workflow, (d) a revised describe-only paragraph that explains the dry-run validation depth and the executed:false invariant, (e) a How To Try It Today bullet naming the execution-policy fields and plan_hash stability, and (f) community-post V1 scope updates plus an updated Explicitly-not-in-V1 line reinforcing that tools/call never executes under --policy.
decisions.rationale: The announcement is the single asset most likely to be read verbatim by adopters and referenced by downstream copy. It must be internally consistent with the 11-command CLI surface, the shipped --policy flag, the dry_run_plan spec, and the local-only invariant.

decisions.statement: Status is needs_human because the launch_ready exit gate explicitly requires human approval before run completion; an agent product_marketing role cannot self-declare gate satisfaction regardless of artifact completeness. All four launch_ready artifacts now cover M20 on HEAD ab55837 (workspace dirty): MESSAGING 10 M20-surface mentions, LAUNCH_PLAN 5, CONTENT_CALENDAR 3, ANNOUNCEMENT 12. Upstream gates (planning_signoff, implementation_complete, qa_ship_verdict) are all passed. proposed_next_role is 'human' so the orchestrator routes to the reviewer, not back to another agent role. run_completion_request is true because the launch phase is the final phase.
decisions.rationale: Launch_ready gate in the run config requires human approval. Following protocol rule 5: propose, do not assume routing authority.

objections.statement: Launch copy still does not show a concrete .tusq/execution-policy.json example payload anywhere. The MESSAGING and ANNOUNCEMENT files describe the fields in prose, but an actual JSON snippet would reduce copy-paste risk during adoption.

verification.evidence_summary: All four launch_ready gate artifacts exist and now carry M20 content: MESSAGING.md 10 M20-surface mentions (Launch Correction bullet, proof sentence, Product Truth serve --policy line, Product Truth execution-policy fields line, Product Truth approval-gate-invariant line, Claims We Can Defend dry-run bullet, three Claims We Must Not Make clarifications), LAUNCH_PLAN.md 5 mentions (Challenge To Prior Framing bullet, must-publish boundary line, optional dry-run workflow example, Expectation-mismatch-on-MCP risk update), CONTENT_CALENDAR.md 3 mentions (launch-day workflow callout, revised proof-path line, Day 3-4 dry-run post), ANNOUNCEMENT.md 12 mentions (step 9, boundary clarification, proof-points paragraph, bash block, describe-only paragraph, how-to-try-it bullet, community-post scope and explicit-not-in-V1 line). summary: Derived M21 (tusq policy init) scaffold-generator increment across all four planning artifacts; preserved local/offline/no-live-execution boundary and safe describe-only default.

decisions.statement: Challenged the intake-template-generated state of .planning/ (empty .planning/distribution-checklist.md, empty .planning/platform-support.md, and site-surface-only additions to .planning/command-surface.md) rather than rubber-stamping them. Those auto-generated stubs are not PM-owned gate artifacts and do not satisfy the M21 planning acceptance contract on their own. Verified baseline HEAD cff7f3e independently: npm test exits 0 (Smoke tests passed; Eval regression harness passed (4 scenarios)); node bin/tusq.js help enumerates the shipped 11-command surface; node bin/tusq.js serve --help includes the shipped --policy flag from M20.
decisions.rationale: The intake flow created three template files but did not derive the M21 increment named in the charter ('reduce manual setup for the opt-in local execution-policy scaffold'). A PM turn that accepted template stubs as planning would fail the acceptance contract item 'PM updates planning artifacts with an M21 increment grounded in VISION.md and current M20 state'.

decisions.statement: M21 scope is exactly one new CLI command: `tusq policy init`. It scaffolds a valid `.tusq/execution-policy.json` file that passes the M20 `loadAndValidatePolicy()` validator byte-for-byte. No interactive wizard, no manifest read, no auto-suggested allowed_capabilities, no multi-environment profiles, no policy migration, no signed-policy work, and absolutely no live API execution under any code path. Rejected three alternatives explicitly: (A) interactive wizard — coupled to TTY state and hostile to CI; (B) a `--init` flag on `tusq serve` — conflates server startup with governance-artifact generation; (C) accepting the V1.2 scope as 'sufficient without a dedicated command' — fails the charter's reduce-manual-setup goal.
decisions.rationale: The charter is narrow by design: one rung of the VISION operator-ergonomics ladder. A small, non-interactive generator under a new `policy` noun leaves room for V2 siblings (`policy suggest`, `policy migrate`, `policy verify`) without later renaming and matches the naming pattern of existing `tusq approve`, `tusq diff`, `tusq docs`, and `tusq compile` commands.

decisions.statement: Default `mode` for a generated policy is `"describe-only"`; `dry-run` requires an explicit `--mode dry-run` flag. This is codified as a new SYSTEM_SPEC.md Constraint 8 (M21 safe-default invariant) and as a separate Key Risk row in ROADMAP.md.
decisions.rationale: If the generator defaulted to dry-run, an operator who ran `tusq policy init` followed by `tusq serve --policy <file>` would silently flip their MCP server from describe-only to dry-run plan emission. That is the exact quiet-governance-change mistake the M20 launch-messaging work (DEC-487, DEC-488) had to correct in the messaging layer; reintroducing it in the tooling layer is unacceptable.

decisions.statement: Generated policy files MUST pass `loadAndValidatePolicy()` byte-for-byte. Smoke coverage will perform a round-trip (generate → validate) to catch any drift between the M21 generator and the M20 validator. This is codified as a new SYSTEM_SPEC.md Constraint 7 (M21 local-only invariant) and enumerated in the Docs and tests required before implementation section.
decisions.rationale: The validator and generator are two independent pieces of logic that describe the same file shape. Without a round-trip test, the two can drift silently — a future contributor could add a field to the validator without updating the generator (or vice versa), and operators would hit cryptic failures at `tusq serve --policy` time. The round-trip collapses both into one enforced contract.

decisions.statement: Marked M20 milestone items as checked in ROADMAP.md to reflect the shipped state at HEAD cff7f3e. Prior planning turn (DEC-466) wrote them unchecked as pre-implementation planning; dev/qa/launch shipped them (DEC-474–DEC-491), but the ROADMAP was never updated to reflect delivery. Leaving them unchecked would mislead a future reviewer about what is shipped vs planned.
decisions.rationale: The planning-artifact contract is truth about state, not a historical log. Verified delivery was independently confirmed this turn by running `npm test` (exits 0 with 4 eval scenarios including the two policy-mode ones from M20) and by inspecting `tusq serve --help` (which shows the shipped `--policy` flag). The check-mark update is a state-of-the-world correction, not a verification claim about the current phase's work.

decisions.statement: Enumerated the docs and tests required before implementation in SYSTEM_SPEC.md under M21 per the intake acceptance contract: smoke coverage for default generation, --mode dry-run, --allowed-capabilities, --force overwrite, exit-1 on pre-existing file without --force, --dry-run no-write behavior, and a validator round-trip; one new eval scenario asserting generated dry-run policy produces the same tools/call dry_run_plan shape as a hand-authored policy; CLI reference docs update; execution-policy.md 'Authoring a policy file' section; README CLI-reference entry for `tusq policy init`. Dev accountability for implementation; QA accountability for independent re-verification as REQ-064–REQ-069 (QA owns the final REQ numbering).
decisions.rationale: The intake acceptance contract explicitly requires 'planning updates identify docs/tests needed before implementation.' Without this enumeration, the dev turn could sign off on implementation_complete with partial coverage, and the QA turn would have to improvise the REQ set from scratch.

decisions.statement: Status is needs_human and proposed_next_role is 'human' because the planning_signoff exit gate for this run explicitly requires human approval before transitioning to the implementation phase. An agent PM cannot self-declare gate satisfaction regardless of artifact completeness. phase_transition_request is null.
decisions.rationale: Protocol Rule 5 says 'Propose the next role, but do not assume routing authority.' The planning_signoff gate's 'Requires human approval' flag (shown in the turn prompt) means the orchestrator must route to the reviewer, not back to an agent role.

objections.statement: The intake-template flow wrote two empty stub files (.planning/distribution-checklist.md, .planning/platform-support.md) that are not PM-owned gate artifacts and do not satisfy the M21 charter. They are retained as-is for this turn because they are non-blocking for the planning_signoff gate and deleting them unprompted would be out of scope for a PM planning turn. A future PM or the template maintainer should decide whether to remove them or fill them in.

decisions.statement: Challenged prior accepted PM turn (turn_521ac8bd2f786742) — it performed planning artifact verification only and cannot satisfy the implementation_complete gate. Fresh dev-authored implementation and runtime verification performed on HEAD abed41f.
decisions.rationale: PM turns derive and verify planning artifacts; they do not ship runtime behavior. The implementation_complete gate requires a dev turn with verified source changes and passing tests.

decisions.statement: Added `cmdPolicy` dispatcher and `cmdPolicyInit` function in src/cli.js implementing the full M21 command surface: --mode (describe-only|dry-run), --reviewer (env-chain resolved), --allowed-capabilities (comma-split, trim, deduplicate), --out, --force, --dry-run, --json, --verbose. Default mode is describe-only per SYSTEM_SPEC Constraint 8. No network, manifest, or target-product I/O was added.
decisions.rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC M21 section specifies this exact command shape. The safe-default invariant (Constraint 8) requires describe-only as the default; dry-run requires an explicit flag. The local-only invariant (Constraint 7) is satisfied because cmdPolicyInit only writes one JSON file and reads no external state.

decisions.statement: Generated policy objects use JSON.stringify(policy, null, 2) + trailing newline, matching the tusq approve write convention. The approved_at field is always stamped at generation time as new Date().toISOString() and is not overridable from the CLI.
decisions.rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC specifies this exact formatting convention so line-diff tooling stays sensible. approved_at is a generation-time audit stamp, not a user-controlled field.

decisions.statement: Added REQ-064 through REQ-068 smoke coverage and a policy-init-generator-round-trip eval scenario. The round-trip (generate → loadAndValidatePolicy via tusq serve --policy) confirms the generator cannot drift from the validator. Eval harness passes 5 scenarios (up from 4).
decisions.rationale: SYSTEM_SPEC Constraint 7 states the generated file MUST pass loadAndValidatePolicy() byte-for-byte. A round-trip test is the only way to enforce this at the integration boundary. The PM planning turn (DEC-495) explicitly required this round-trip in the Docs and tests required section.

decisions.statement: Challenged the prior accepted turn (turn_8fa593ad7654ffa5, role=qa, HEAD be1164b) rather than rubber-stamping its SHIP verdict. The QA turn did not modify launch artifacts and the last product_marketing pass covered only through M20.
